(*Boarisch* or *Bairisch*;[[2]](#cite_note-2) [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Bayrisch* [[ˈbaɪʁɪʃ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Bairisch.ogg "File:Bairisch.ogg")), alternately **Austro-Bavarian**, is a group of [Upper German](/wiki/Upper_German "Upper German") [varieties](/wiki/Variety_(linguistics) "Variety (linguistics)") spoken in the south-east of the [German language](/wiki/German_language "German language") area, including the German state of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), most of Austria, and [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol") in Italy.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowley2011300-3) Prior to 1945, Bavarian was also prevalent in parts of the southern [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland") and western Hungary.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Bavarian is spoken by approximately 12 million people in an area of around 125,000 square kilometres (48,000 sq mi), making it the largest of all [German dialects](/wiki/German_dialects "German dialects"). In 2008, 45 percent of Bavarians claimed to use only dialect in everyday communication.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowley2011-5)

**Bavarian** (*Boarisch* or *Bairisch*;[[2]](#cite_note-2) [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"): *Bayrisch* [[ˈbaɪʁɪʃ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Standard_German "Help:IPA/Standard German") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Bairisch.ogg "File:Bairisch.ogg")), alternately **Austro-Bavarian**, is a group of [Upper German](/wiki/Upper_German "Upper German") [varieties](/wiki/Variety_(linguistics) "Variety (linguistics)") spoken in the south-east of the [German language](/wiki/German_language "German language") area, including the German state of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"), most of Austria, and [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol") in Italy.[[3]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowley2011300-3) Prior to 1945, Bavarian was also prevalent in parts of the southern [Sudetenland](/wiki/Sudetenland "Sudetenland") and western Hungary.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Bavarian is spoken by approximately 12 million people in an area of around 125,000 square kilometres (48,000 sq mi), making it the largest of all [German dialects](/wiki/German_dialects "German dialects"). In 2008, 45 percent of Bavarians claimed to use only dialect in everyday communication.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowley2011-5)

## Language or dialect

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Language or dialect")]

Bavarian is generally considered to be a dialect of [German](/wiki/German_language "German language"),[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) but many sources classify it as a separate language: the [International Organization for Standardization](/wiki/International_Organization_for_Standardization "International Organization for Standardization") has assigned a unique [ISO 639-3](/wiki/ISO_639-3 "ISO 639-3") language code (*bar*),[[9]](#cite_note-:0-9), the [Glottolog](/wiki/Glottolog "Glottolog") has given separate language code (*bava1246*) and the [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") lists Bavarian in the *[Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger](/wiki/Atlas_of_the_World%27s_Languages_in_Danger "Atlas of the World's Languages in Danger")* since 2009; however, the classification of Bavarian as an *individual language* has been criticized by some scholars of Bavarian.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowley2011301–302-10)[[11]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEibl201496-11)

Reasons why Bavarian can be viewed as a dialect of German include the perception of its speakers, the lack of standardization, the traditional use of Standard German as a [roofing language](/wiki/Dachsprache "Dachsprache"), the relative closeness to German which does not justify Bavarian to be viewed as an [abstand language](/wiki/Abstand_language "Abstand language"), or the fact that no country applied for Bavarian to be entered into the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages").[[12]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERowley2011301–307-12)[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEEibl201490–91,_96-13)

The difference between Bavarian and [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") is larger than the difference between Danish and some varieties of Norwegian or between Czech and Slovak.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Origins

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Origins")]

### History and etymology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History and etymology")]

Further information: [History of Bavaria](/wiki/History_of_Bavaria "History of Bavaria")

The word *Bavarian* is derived from the name of the [Baiuvarii](/wiki/Baiuvarii "Baiuvarii") people who first appeared under this name in Bavaria in the 6th century. The origin of their name is uncertain, but the most common theory reconstructs the word as *\*Bajowarjōz*, meaning 'inhabitants of [Boii](/wiki/Boii "Boii") land'. The Boii were [Celtic](/wiki/Celts "Celts") inhabitants of the area before the Roman conquest, and their name survived.[[15]](#cite_note-barbrite-15)

The local population eventually established the [Duchy of Bavaria](/wiki/Duchy_of_Bavaria "Duchy of Bavaria"), forming the south-eastern part of the [kingdom of Germany](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Germany "Kingdom of Germany"). The [Old High German](/wiki/Old_High_German "Old High German") documents from the area of Bavaria are identified as *Altbairisch* (Old Bavarian), even though at this early date there were few distinctive features that would divide it from [Alemannic German](/wiki/Alemannic_German "Alemannic German").

The dialectal separation of Upper German into East Upper German (Bavarian) and West Upper German (Alemannic) became more tangible in the [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German") period, from about the 12th century.

## Geographical distribution and dialects

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Geographical distribution and dialects")]

* In Europe:
  + In Germany, the language is spoken in [Upper Bavaria](/wiki/Upper_Bavaria "Upper Bavaria"), [Lower Bavaria](/wiki/Lower_Bavaria "Lower Bavaria"), and the [Upper Palatinate](/wiki/Upper_Palatinate "Upper Palatinate") districts in [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria"). It is also spoken in southern [Vogtland](/wiki/Vogtland "Vogtland"), in [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony");
  + In Austria, except [Vorarlberg](/wiki/Vorarlberg "Vorarlberg");
  + In Italy in [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol") and a handful of linguistic enclaves of Cimbrian and Carnic people in [Northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy");
  + In Switzerland, it is spoken in the village of [Samnaun](/wiki/Samnaun "Samnaun"), in [Grisons](/wiki/Grisons "Grisons");
  + In [Sopron](/wiki/Sopron "Sopron") (Hungary) and surroundings.
* Outside of Europe:
  + In [Treze Tílias](/wiki/Treze_T%C3%ADlias "Treze Tílias"), Brazil
  + In [Pozuzo, Peru](/wiki/Pozuzo "Pozuzo")
  + In [the United States and Canada](/wiki/Hutterite_German "Hutterite German") (Hutterite German)

Three main dialects of Bavarian are:

* [Northern Bavarian](/wiki/Northern_Bavarian "Northern Bavarian"), mainly spoken in [Upper Palatinate](/wiki/Upper_Palatinate "Upper Palatinate"), but also in adjacent areas (small parts of [Upper Franconia](/wiki/Upper_Franconia "Upper Franconia") ([Wunsiedel district](/wiki/Wunsiedel_(district) "Wunsiedel (district)") and [Bayreuth district](/wiki/Bayreuth_(district) "Bayreuth (district)")), [Saxony](/wiki/Saxony "Saxony") (southern [Vogtland](/wiki/Vogtland "Vogtland")), [Middle Franconia](/wiki/Middle_Franconia "Middle Franconia"), [Upper Bavaria](/wiki/Upper_Bavaria "Upper Bavaria") and [Lower Bavaria](/wiki/Lower_Bavaria "Lower Bavaria")).
* [Central Bavarian](/wiki/Central_Bavarian "Central Bavarian") along the main rivers [Isar](/wiki/Isar "Isar") and Danube, spoken in [Upper Bavaria](/wiki/Upper_Bavaria "Upper Bavaria") (including Munich, which has a standard German-speaking majority), [Lower Bavaria](/wiki/Lower_Bavaria "Lower Bavaria"), southern [Upper Palatinate](/wiki/Upper_Palatinate "Upper Palatinate"), the [Swabian](/wiki/Swabia_(Bavaria) "Swabia (Bavaria)") district of [Aichach-Friedberg](/wiki/Aichach-Friedberg "Aichach-Friedberg"), the northern parts of the [State of Salzburg](/wiki/Salzburg_(state) "Salzburg (state)"), [Upper Austria](/wiki/Upper_Austria "Upper Austria"), [Lower Austria](/wiki/Lower_Austria "Lower Austria"), [Vienna](/wiki/Vienna "Vienna") (*see [Viennese German](/wiki/Viennese_German "Viennese German")*) and the Northern [Burgenland](/wiki/Burgenland "Burgenland").
* [Southern Bavarian](/wiki/Southern_Bavarian "Southern Bavarian") in [Samnaun](/wiki/Samnaun "Samnaun"), [Tyrol](/wiki/Tyrol_(state) "Tyrol (state)"), [South Tyrol](/wiki/South_Tyrol "South Tyrol"), [Carinthia](/wiki/Carinthia "Carinthia"), [Styria](/wiki/Styria "Styria"), and the southern parts of Salzburg and Burgenland.

Differences are clearly noticeable within those three subgroups, which in Austria often coincide with the borders of the particular states. For example, each of the accents of Carinthia, Styria, and Tyrol can be easily recognised. Also, there is a marked difference between eastern and western central Bavarian, roughly coinciding with the border between Austria and Bavaria. In addition, the Viennese dialect has some characteristics distinguishing it from all other dialects. In Vienna, minor, but recognizable, variations are characteristic for distinct districts of the city.

Before the [expulsion of Germans from Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Expulsion_of_Germans_from_Czechoslovakia "Expulsion of Germans from Czechoslovakia"), the linguistic border of Bavarian with Czech was on the farther side of the [Bohemian Forest](/wiki/Bohemian_Forest "Bohemian Forest") and its Bohemian foreland was Bavarian-speaking.

Alternatively, there are four main dialects:[[16]](#cite_note-16)

* North Bavarian
* Middle Bavarian
* South Middle Bavarian
* South Bavarian

## Use

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Use")]

For the use of Bavarian and standard German in Austria, see [Austrian German](/wiki/Austrian_German "Austrian German").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Rottach-Egern_-_Kramer_Lad%E2%80%99l_-_Essen_ist_ein_Bed%C3%BCrfnis.jpg/250px-Rottach-Egern_-_Kramer_Lad%E2%80%99l_-_Essen_ist_ein_Bed%C3%BCrfnis.jpg)](/wiki/File:Rottach-Egern_-_Kramer_Lad%E2%80%99l_-_Essen_ist_ein_Bed%C3%BCrfnis.jpg)

Public sign combining Standard German and Bavarian

Bavarian has no official status in any country or territory. Bavarian differs sufficiently from [Standard German](/wiki/Standard_German "Standard German") to make it difficult for native speakers to adopt standard pronunciation. Educated [Bavarians](/wiki/Bavarians "Bavarians") and Austrians can almost always read, write and understand Standard German, but they may have little opportunity to speak it, especially in rural areas. In those regions, Standard German is restricted to use as the language of writing and the media. It is therefore often referred to as *Schriftdeutsch* ("written German") rather than the usual term *Hochdeutsch* ("[High German](/wiki/High_German_languages "High German languages")" or "Standard German"). Given that [Central German](/wiki/Central_German "Central German") and Upper German together comprise the [High German languages](/wiki/High_German_languages "High German languages"), out of which the then new, written standard was developed and as opposed to [Low German](/wiki/Low_German "Low German"), that is an alternative naming many High German dialect speakers regard justified.

### School

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: School")]

Bavaria and Austria officially use Standard German as the primary medium of education. With the spread of universal education, the exposure of speakers of Bavarian to Standard German has been increasing, and many younger people, especially in the region's cities and larger towns, speak Standard German with only a slight accent. This accent usually only exists in families where Bavarian is spoken regularly. Families that do not use Bavarian at home usually use Standard German instead. In Austria, some parts of grammar and spelling are taught in Standard German lessons.
As reading and writing in Bavarian is generally not taught at schools, almost all literate speakers of the language prefer to use Standard German for writing. Regional authors and literature may play a role in education as well, but by and large, Standard German is the *[lingua franca](/wiki/Lingua_franca "Lingua franca")*.

### Literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Literature")]

Although there exist grammars, vocabularies, and a translation of the Bible[[17]](#cite_note-17) in Bavarian, there is no common orthographic standard. Poetry is written in various Bavarian dialects, and many pop songs use the language as well, especially ones belonging to the [Austropop](/wiki/Austropop "Austropop") wave of the 1970s and 1980s.

Although Bavarian as a spoken language is in daily use in its region, Standard German, often with strong regional influence, is preferred in the mass media.

[Ludwig Thoma](/wiki/Ludwig_Thoma "Ludwig Thoma") was a noted German author who wrote works such as *Lausbubengeschichten* in Bavarian.

### Web

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Web")]

There is a [Bavarian Wikipedia](https://bar.wikipedia.org/wiki/ "bar:"). Also, the official [FC Bayern Munich](/wiki/FC_Bayern_Munich "FC Bayern Munich") website was available in Bavarian.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

Notes:

* Aspiration may occur among voiceless plosives in word-initial position.
* The phoneme /h/ is frequently realised as [[ç](/wiki/Voiceless_palatal_fricative "Voiceless palatal fricative")] or [[x](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_fricative "Voiceless velar fricative")] word-internally and is realised as [[h](/wiki/Voiceless_glottal_fricative "Voiceless glottal fricative")] word-initially.
* Intervocalic /s/ can be voiced to [[z](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_fricative "Voiced alveolar fricative")], unless it is *fortis* (lengthened), such as in /hɔasːn/ 'to be named', compared to /rɔasn/ 'to travel', where the sibilant is *lenis*.
* Realization of trill sounds varies by dialect. Some (especially those close to the Czech and Swiss border) realize it as a alveolar trill ([r](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_trill "Voiced alveolar trill")) or sometimes tap ([ɾ](/wiki/Voiced_alveolar_tap_or_flap "Voiced alveolar tap or flap")). Others such as almost all southern Bavarian dialects realize either as a Uvular trill ([ʀ](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_trill "Voiced uvular trill")) or fricative ([ʁ](/wiki/Voiced_uvular_fricative "Voiced uvular fricative")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]
* Intervocalic /v/ can be realised as [[ʋ](/wiki/Voiced_labiodental_approximant "Voiced labiodental approximant")] or [[β](/wiki/Voiced_bilabial_fricative "Voiced bilabial fricative"), [w](/wiki/Voiced_labial%E2%80%93velar_approximant "Voiced labial–velar approximant")].
* Some dialects, such as the Bavarian dialect in South Tyrol, realise /k/ as an affricate [[k͡x](/wiki/Voiceless_velar_affricate "Voiceless velar affricate")] word-initially and before /m, n, l, r/, which is an extension of the [High German consonant shift](/wiki/High_German_consonant_shift "High German consonant shift") to velar consonants.

Bavarian has an extensive vowel inventory, like most Germanic languages. Vowels can be grouped as back rounded, front unrounded and front rounded. They are also traditionally distinguished by [length](/wiki/Vowel_length "Vowel length") or [tenseness](/wiki/Tenseness "Tenseness").

## Grammar

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Grammar")]

* Northern and Central Bavarian usually have case inflection only for the article. With few exceptions, nouns are not inflected for case.
* The [simple past](/wiki/Preterite "Preterite") tense is rare in Bavarian and has been retained for only a few verbs, including 'to be' and 'to want'. In general, the perfect is used to express past time.
* Bavarian features verbal inflection for several moods such as [indicative](/wiki/Indicative "Indicative"), [subjunctive](/wiki/Subjunctive "Subjunctive"), [imperative](/wiki/Imperative_mood "Imperative mood") and [optative](/wiki/Optative "Optative"). See the table below for inflection of the Bavarian verb *måcha*, 'make; do':

| *måcha* | Indicative | Imperative | Subjunctive | Optative |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1. Sg | *i måch* | — | *i måchad* | *måchadi* |
| 2. Sg (informal) | *du måchst* | *måch!* | *du måchast* | *måchast* |
| 3. Sg | *er måcht* | *er måch!* | *er måchad* | *måchada* |
| 1. Pl | *mia måchan\** | *måchma!* | *mia måchadn* | *måchadma* |
| 2. Pl | *eß måchts* | *måchts!* | *eß måchats* | *måchats* |
| 3. Pl | *se måchan(t)* | — | *se måchadn* | *måchadns* |
| 2. Sg (formal) | *Si måchan* | *måchan’S!* | *Si måchadn* | *måchadn’S* |

### Pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Pronouns")]

#### Personal pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Personal pronouns")]

|  | Singular | | | | Plural | | |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  | 1st person | 2nd person informal | 2nd person formal | 3rd person | 1st person | 2nd person | 3rd person |
| Nominative | *i* | *du* | *Si* | *ea*, *se*/*de*, *des* | *mia* | *eß*/*öß* / *ia\** | *se* |
| *Unstressed* | *i* | -- | *-'S* | *-a*, *-'s*, *-'s* | *-ma* | *-'s* | *-'s* |
| Dative | *mia* | *dia* | *Eana* | *eam*, *eara*/*iara*, *dem* | *uns*, *ins* | *enk* / *eich\** | *ea*, *eana* |
| *Unstressed* | *-ma* | *-da* |  |  |  |  |  |
| Accusative | *-mi* | *-di* | *Eana* | *eam*, *eara*/*iara*, *des* | *uns*, *ins* | *enk* / *eich\** | *ea*, *eana* |
| *Unstressed* |  |  | *Si* | *-'n*, ..., *-'s* |  |  | *-'s* |

\* These are typically used in the northernmost dialects of Bavarian.

#### Possessive pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Possessive pronouns")]

|  | Masculine singular | | Feminine singular | | Neuter singular | | Plural (any gender) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Nominative | mei | meina | mei | meine | mei | mei(n)s | meine |
| Accusative | mein | |
| Dative | meim | | meina | | meim | |

The possessive pronouns *Deina* and *Seina* inflect in the same manner. Oftentimes, *-nige* is added to the nominative to form the adjective form of the possessive pronoun, like mei(nige), dei(nige), and the like.

#### Indefinite pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Indefinite pronouns")]

Just like the possessive pronouns listed above, the indefinite pronouns *koana*, "none", and *oana*, "one" are inflected the same way.

There is also the indefinite pronoun *ebba(d)*, "someone" with its impersonal form *ebb(a)s*, "something". It is inflected in the following way:

|  | Personal | Impersonal |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Nominative | ebba | ebbs |
| Accusative | ebban |
| Dative | ebbam | |

#### Interrogative pronouns

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Interrogative pronouns")]

The interrogative pronouns *wea*, "who", and *wås*, "what" are inflected the same way the indefinite pronoun *ebba* is inflected.

|  | Personal | Impersonal |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Nominative | wea | wås |
| Accusative | wen |
| Dative | wem | |

## Society

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Bavarian_language&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Society")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| [icon](/wiki/File:Question_book-new.svg) | This section **needs additional citations for [verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")**. Please help [improve this article](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Bavarian_language "Special:EditPage/Bavarian language") by [adding citations to reliable sources](/wiki/Help:Referencing_for_beginners "Help:Referencing for beginners") in this section. Unsourced material may be challenged and removed. *(July 2021)* *([Learn how and when to remove this message](/wiki/Help:Maintenance_template_removal "Help:Maintenance template removal"))* |

Bavarians produce a variety of nicknames for those who bear traditional Bavarian or German names like Josef, Theresa or Georg (becoming *Sepp'l* or more commonly *Sepp*, *Resi* and *Schorsch*, respectively). Bavarians often refer to names with the family name coming first (like *da Stoiber Ede* instead of [Edmund Stoiber](/wiki/Edmund_Stoiber "Edmund Stoiber")). The use of the article is considered mandatory when using this linguistic variation. In addition, nicknames different from the family name exist for almost all families, especially in small villages. They consist largely of their profession, names or professions of deceased inhabitants of their homes or the site where their homes are located. This nickname is called *Hausname* (en: name of the house) and is seldom used to name the person, but more to state where they come from or live or to whom they are related. Examples of this are:

* Mohler (e.g. *Maler* – painter)
* Bachbauer (farmer who lives near a brook/creek)
* Moosrees (Theresa (Rees/Resi) who lives near a moss)
* Schreiner (joiner/carpenter)
